# Extracao: transcription_mp4_02-The_First_Week_of_Ownership

Congratulations, you just bought your first business. Now what do you do? So let me walk you through a quick list of things that you're going to want to prioritize and do starting day one. First thing you're going to want to do is you're going to want to meet all your employees. You're going to want to do an assessment of those employees. Who's your highest contributors? Who's your lowest distractors? You're going to want to find out who your NIOPs are, negative influence of other people. You're going to want to find out who your PIOPs are, your most positive people. And you're going to want to rally your positive people around establishing targets and objectives and initiatives. Okay? First thing you're going to want to do. The second thing you're going to want to do is you're going to want to call all of your customers. In fact, you should have the exiting business owner sit on those calls with you and call those customers and introduce you to them. It should be something that you ask them to do post closing. You're going to want to introduce who you are and your vision for your organization. So you're going to want them to have a sense of security that you're just like with the bankers, just like with the financiers, just like with the other people selling. You're going to want to send a sense of confidence that you know what you're doing. Okay? The third thing you're going to want to do is you're going to want to sit with that business owner that just sold to you and you're going to want to call all your vendors. Okay? The people that you're reliant on buying your products or your services from that are supporting your business. You want them to know that you're the new owner, you're in control, introduce yourself, give them your vision. So it's really important here to have a vision with your employees, with your customers, and with your vendors. Okay? So everyone understands who you are, where you're going, and what your intention is. You're going to want to introduce your 30, 60, 90 day integration plan for the business, culturally, operationally, and financially with your team, which we're going to get into in another module. So when you first walk in that business and introduce yourself to your employees, your customers, and your vendors, you're going to want to send a sense of confidence, just like you had to build with your financiers, just like you had to build with the business owner to be willing to sell to you. So it's really important to paint the biggest picture possible. That vision has to be significant, and you have to give people confidence that you're committed to that. If you demonstrate that on your first day, every day after that will become a lot easier. Now, what else would I do? When I reach out to my clients, I would also find out who owes me the most amount of money. I would know who my most important clients are and who owes me money. They may think, by the way, that since there's a new buyer, you'll forget they owe money to the business. So you want to want to make sure that when you introduce yourself, you also let them know that you're aware of the business relationship you have with them, and you're looking forward to going deeper in that. So follow the money. I want to know when I take a business over, how much are we generating every single day? How much are we spending every single day? And what is the targets for the business every single day? You're going to want to start tracking that because if you're tracking that daily, monthly target broken down by daily objectives, and if you're tracking how much you're generating in cash and collecting every single day towards target, you'll have a very good algorithm that you'll develop inside the business over 90 days so that you can predict what's happening for that particular month. So those are the first things I would do as a new business owner. In the next lesson, we're going to revisit your exit plan so that you can tailor your business on the entry to maximize your value on the exit.
